model_narrative: We ship a product from several canning plants to several markets. Each plant-to-market route has a known shipping cost per case that grows with distance. We decide how many cases to ship on each route, and we allow some of a market's demand to go unfilled at a penalty. The objective is to make the total cost as small as possible, counting both the shipping cost and the penalty for unfilled demand.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the canning plants that ship the product

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the markets that receive the product

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the demand scenarios; not used by any constraint in this version

params.name: f
params.doc: freight rate in dollars per case per thousand miles

params.name: pen
params.doc: penalty charged per case of unfilled demand, in thousands of dollars per case

params.name: a
params.doc: shipping capacity of each plant, in cases

params.name: b
params.doc: demand of each market, in cases

params.name: d
params.doc: distance from a plant to a market, in thousands of miles

params.name: c
params.doc: shipping cost per case on a plant-to-market route, in thousands of dollars; it is the freight rate times the distance divided by one thousand

params.name: bmult
params.doc: random demand multiplier for each scenario; not used by any constraint in this version

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the number of cases shipped from a plant to a market

vars.name: slack
vars.doc: the number of cases of demand left unfilled at a market

vars.name: z
vars.doc: total transportation cost in thousands of dollars

constraints.name: z
constraints.intent: The total cost has two pieces. The first is the per-case shipping cost on each route multiplied by the cases shipped on it, summed over every plant and market. The second charges the unfilled-demand penalty per case for every case left unfilled, summed over all markets. Set the total cost variable equal to the sum of those two pieces.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: No plant can ship out more than its capacity. For each plant, the total cases sent from it to all markets must not exceed that plant's capacity.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Each market's demand must be covered by what arrives plus whatever is left unfilled. For each market, the total cases shipped into it from all plants together with the unfilled amount at that market must be at least the market's demand.

query: Carrier contracts cap any single plant-to-market lane at 250 cases a month — beyond that we'd be into spot-rate territory we haven't budgeted for. There's a proposal to hold every lane to that 250-case ceiling. Plug it in and tell me what the optimizer returns for cost.

query: Seattle's dumping its entire 350 cases on just two markets and skipping Topeka entirely. Couldn't we require that no plant sends more than 60% of its capacity to any single market? Wouldn't a more spread-out plan stay competitive on cost?

query: Seattle is sitting out two of the three markets entirely — it never touches New York or Topeka. Shouldn't Seattle carry at least 20% of every market's order so we're not single-sourced anywhere? What's keeping the model from spreading Seattle around like that?

query: Half the plant-to-market grid is bone dry — most lanes ship zero. Shouldn't every lane carry at least a token 40 cases so each route stays warm and we're not caught flat if one collapses? What's the model seeing that makes it leave so many lanes idle?
